candidate.test_code:
rows = [[1], [], [2], [3]]
result = report(rows)["kept"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == [[1], [2], [3]], "no populated row should be lost"
candidate.context_note: Cleans a batch of parsed spreadsheet rows before they are rendered.

reason_options.text: The loop deletes from the very list it is walking, so everything after the deletion shifts down one place and the element that lands in the vacated index is never seen
remove() deletes every matching element, not just the first one
continue skips the rest of the function, so kept is never appended to again
An empty list is truthy, so the guard inside the loop never runs

draft_explanation.summary: The blank rows are already excluded by the continue, so nothing needs deleting. The deletion is pure damage: it shortens the list the for-loop is walking, every later element slides down one index, and the loop's counter marches straight past whatever landed in the gap.
draft_explanation.principle: Never delete from a sequence you are iterating. If you are building a new list anyway, you do not need to.

line_notes.note: This shortens the list the loop is walking, and the loop counter does not notice.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A populated row sits immediately after a blank one.
candidate.divergence_input: rows = [[1], [], [2], [3]] passed to report(), reading the kept entry.
candidate.divergence_justification: Deleting the blank row at index 1 slides [2] down into index 1, which the loop has already passed, so [2] is never appended to kept and vanishes from the output.

candidate.test_code:
result = pack([{"label": "caf\u00e9-latte"}])[0]
print(repr(result))
assert result is None, "the label overruns a ten-byte field"
candidate.context_note: Prepares labels for a binary record format that budgets ten bytes per field.

reason_options.text: The length is taken from the unencoded string, so a character that costs more than one byte in UTF-8 slips past a budget that is measured in bytes
encode() rewrites the string in place, so len() already sees the encoded form
UTF-8 spends exactly two bytes on every character, so the count is always double what len() reports
Returning None from pack_field is what breaks the caller, which cannot handle a missing entry

draft_explanation.summary: The line above produces the bytes that will actually be written, but the guard measures the string those bytes came from. A str counts characters; the field counts bytes. UTF-8 spends one byte on ASCII and two or more on anything else, so an over-long label sails through the check and is silently written past the end of its field.
draft_explanation.principle: Measure the thing you are about to store, in the unit the destination counts.

line_notes.note: The encoded bytes are sitting right above; this measures the string instead.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A label whose character count fits the budget but whose UTF-8 byte count does not.
candidate.divergence_input: A single record whose label is a ten-character string containing one accented character, eleven bytes once encoded.
candidate.divergence_justification: The label is ten characters, so the character count clears a ten-unit budget and the bytes are handed back for writing. It is eleven bytes encoded, so the corrected guard rejects it.

candidate.context_note: Keeps only the words that appear often enough to be worth reporting.

reason_options.text: The dictionary is resized while it is being walked, and the iterator refuses to continue over a container that changed size underneath it
dict.get(word, 0) returns None for a word that has not been seen, so the addition raises TypeError
Iterating a dictionary yields its values, so counts[key] is a lookup by count rather than by word
continue exits the loop entirely, so only the first word is ever counted

draft_explanation.summary: The rare entries are already excluded by the continue, so the deletion achieves nothing except to change the size of the dictionary mid-walk. CPython detects that and raises RuntimeError on the next step, so the whole call dies rather than returning a slightly wrong answer.
draft_explanation.principle: Never add to or delete from a dictionary you are iterating. Walk a snapshot of the keys, or build a new dictionary.

line_notes.note: This resizes the dictionary that the loop above is walking.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: Any word whose count falls below the floor while other words remain to be visited.
candidate.divergence_input: frequent(["a", "a", "b", "c", "c", "c"], 2) - the word "b" falls below the floor.
candidate.divergence_justification: Deleting the entry for b changes the size of counts, so the next step of the walk raises RuntimeError and the caller never receives a result.

candidate.test_code:
result = scoreboard([{"name": "ada", "score": 91.5}])["lines"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == ["ada scored 91.5"], "a half point should survive rendering"
candidate.context_note: Renders a leaderboard line for each competitor. Scores carry one decimal place.

reason_options.text: The integer conversion truncates the fractional part of a score that is not whole
The percent operator needs a list, not a tuple, so the arguments are never substituted
A dictionary lookup inside a format expression is evaluated lazily, so both fields hold the last row
len(rows) counts characters rather than entries, so the entry count is inflated

draft_explanation.summary: The %d conversion asks for an integer. Handed a float it does not complain; it quietly truncates towards zero. Every half point in the competition disappears from the board and nothing anywhere reports an error.
draft_explanation.principle: A conversion specifier is a coercion, not an assertion. Match it to the type you actually hold.

line_notes.note: This conversion takes the whole part of the score and discards the rest.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: Any score that is not a whole number.
candidate.divergence_justification: 91.5 rendered through %d becomes 91. The board silently understates every fractional score.

candidate.test_code:
result = describe(build_all("users", ["O'Brien"]))["queries"][0]
print(repr(result))
assert result == "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'O''Brien'", "the caller value must be quoted"
candidate.context_note: Builds the read-only report queries that a downstream tool executes.

reason_options.text: The caller value is pasted straight between the quotes, so a value containing a quote character closes the literal early and the rest is read as query text
String concatenation with the plus operator raises TypeError when either side is not a string
The table name is interpolated the same way, and that is the only untrusted part of the query
replace() substitutes only the first occurrence, so quote() would not help even if it were called

draft_explanation.summary: quote() exists and is never called. The caller's value is concatenated straight into the literal, so a single quote inside it terminates the literal and everything after it is parsed as part of the statement. The helper right above shows what should have happened to it.
draft_explanation.principle: Never concatenate an untrusted value into a query. Bind it, or at minimum escape it.

line_notes.note: The caller value goes straight between the quote marks, unescaped.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A value containing a single quote.
candidate.divergence_input: A name of O'Brien, which contains one apostrophe.
candidate.divergence_justification: The apostrophe in the name closes the string literal, so the query the downstream tool receives is not the query that was intended.

candidate.test_code:
result = quote({"sku": "A1", "base": 10}, [1, 2])
print(repr(result))
assert result == [10, 20], "a different multiplier should be priced, not served from cache"
candidate.context_note: Prices an item at several customer tiers. The cache exists because base lookups are expensive.

reason_options.text: The cache key ignores the multiplier, so a result computed for one tier is served for every other tier
The cache is a module-level dictionary, and module-level state cannot persist between calls
A list comprehension evaluates lazily, so every element ends up holding the last multiplier
Membership testing with in scans the values of a dictionary, not its keys

draft_explanation.summary: A memo key must capture every input the result depends on. This one captures the item and forgets the multiplier, so the first tier priced wins forever and every subsequent tier is answered with a value computed for someone else.
draft_explanation.principle: The cache key must include every argument the cached value depends on. If it does not, the cache is a source of wrong answers.

line_notes.note: The result depends on the multiplier, and the key does not mention it.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: The same item is priced at two different multipliers.
candidate.divergence_input: quote({"sku": "A1", "base": 10}, [1, 2]) - the same item priced at two tiers.
candidate.divergence_justification: The first tier is priced and stored under a key that mentions only the item. The second tier finds that key already present and is served the first tier's price.

candidate.test_code:
result = audit(["caf\u00e9"])
print(repr(result))
assert result == [4], "a round trip should hand back what it was given"
candidate.context_note: Carries text over a byte channel and reads it back on the far side.

reason_options.text: The bytes are written in one encoding and read back in another, so a multi-byte character comes back as the several separate characters its bytes happen to spell
encode() and decode() are inverses regardless of the codec named, so the codec argument has no effect
len() on a string returns its byte count, so the two sides can never agree
A list comprehension cannot call a function that raises, so audit() silently returns an empty list

draft_explanation.summary: The writer spends two bytes on the accented character; the reader is told each byte is a whole character in a single-byte codec. Nothing raises. The text simply comes back one character longer than it went in, and the corruption travels quietly downstream.
draft_explanation.principle: The codec that reads must be the codec that wrote. A mismatch is silent, not fatal.

line_notes.note: This reads back in a different codec from the one that wrote.

candidate.divergence_input: A single four-character message containing one accented character, five bytes in UTF-8.
candidate.divergence_justification: The accented character is two bytes in UTF-8. Read back as latin-1 those two bytes become two separate characters, so a four-character message returns as five.

candidate.test_code:
import datetime

east = datetime.timezone(datetime.timedelta(hours=5))
start = datetime.datetime(2026, 1, 1, 12, 0, tzinfo=east)
end = datetime.datetime(2026, 1, 1, 8, 0, tzinfo=UTC)
result = window(start, end)
print(repr(result))
assert result == 60, "noon in a plus-five zone is 07:00 UTC, one hour before the end"
candidate.context_note: Measures how long a job has left, given a start in the operator's zone and a deadline in UTC.

reason_options.text: Replacing the zone relabels the wall-clock reading as UTC instead of converting it, so the instant silently moves by the size of the offset
Two aware datetimes cannot be subtracted, so the subtraction raises TypeError
total_seconds() returns whole minutes already, so the floor division divides by sixty twice
datetime.timezone.utc is a naive zone, so every value built from it is naive

draft_explanation.summary: replace() edits the label on the reading and leaves the digits alone: noon in a plus-five zone becomes noon UTC, an instant five hours earlier than the one the operator meant. astimezone() moves the digits so the instant is preserved.
draft_explanation.principle: replace(tzinfo=...) relabels. astimezone() converts. Only one of them preserves the instant.

line_notes.note: This changes the label without moving the reading, so the instant shifts by the offset.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A start whose zone offset is not zero.
candidate.divergence_input: A start of noon in a UTC-plus-five zone and a deadline of 08:00 UTC.
candidate.divergence_justification: The true start is 07:00 UTC, one hour before the deadline. Relabelling makes it 12:00 UTC, four hours after, so the remaining window is reported as negative.

candidate.test_code:
result = latency_report([10, 11])["mean"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == 10.5, "the mean of ten and eleven is ten and a half"
candidate.context_note: Summarises a window of request latencies in milliseconds.

reason_options.text: The floor operator throws away the fractional part, so a mean that is not whole is reported low
sum() on a list of integers returns a float, so the division is already exact
max() and min() are evaluated in the wrong order, so the spread comes back negative
Dividing an integer by an integer in Python always truncates, whichever operator is used

draft_explanation.summary: Two slashes is floor division: it discards the remainder and returns the whole part. Averaging ten and eleven gives ten and a half, and the report says ten. Every mean in the system is biased downward and nothing announces it.
draft_explanation.principle: Two slashes floors. One slash divides. Choose the one that matches the quantity you are reporting.

line_notes.note: This discards the remainder rather than keeping it.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: Any window whose mean is not a whole number.
candidate.divergence_input: A window of two samples, ten and eleven milliseconds.
candidate.divergence_justification: The true mean is 10.5. Floor division reports 10, understating every non-whole average in the report.

candidate.test_code:
result = run_pipeline([1, 10], 0)
print(repr(result))
assert result == [1, 10], "each step should carry the offset it was built with"
candidate.context_note: Builds one transform per configured offset, then runs them all against a seed value.

reason_options.text: Every lambda closes over the loop variable itself rather than its value at the time, so all of them read whatever it held when the loop finished
A lambda cannot refer to a name from the enclosing function, so offset is undefined when the step runs
append() stores a copy of the lambda, so each step in the list is the same object
The loop variable is deleted when the loop ends, so calling a step raises NameError

draft_explanation.summary: A closure captures the variable, not a snapshot of it. All the lambdas share the single loop variable, and by the time any of them runs the loop has finished and left the last offset behind. Every step applies that one.
draft_explanation.principle: Closures capture names, not values. Bind the value at definition time, with a default argument or a factory.

line_notes.note: This lambda reads the loop variable when it runs, not when it is built.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: More than one offset, and the steps are called after the loop has finished.
candidate.divergence_input: Offsets of 1 and 10, run against a seed of 0.
candidate.divergence_justification: Both lambdas read the same loop variable, which holds 10 once the loop ends, so both add 10 and the first offset is lost.

candidate.test_code:
first = tag(instantiate(TEMPLATE, "alpha"), "urgent")
second = instantiate(TEMPLATE, "beta")
result = second["tags"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == ["draft"], "a fresh record should carry only the template tags"
candidate.context_note: Stamps out records from a shared template, then lets callers tag them.

reason_options.text: A shallow copy duplicates the outer dictionary but not the list inside it, so both records point at one list of tags
copy.copy() returns the original object unchanged, so instantiate() hands back the template itself
Assigning to made["name"] rebinds the template's name as well, because strings are mutable
append() returns a new list, so tag() discards its own result

draft_explanation.summary: A shallow copy is one level deep. The new dictionary is genuinely new, so writing the name is safe, but every value inside it is the same object as before. The tags list is shared with the template, so tagging one record edits the template every later record is stamped from.
draft_explanation.principle: A shallow copy duplicates the container, not what it contains. For nested mutable structures, copy deeply.

line_notes.note: This duplicates the outer dictionary only; the list inside is still shared.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A record is tagged, and then a second record is stamped from the same template.
candidate.divergence_input: One record tagged urgent, then a second record instantiated and its tags read.
candidate.divergence_justification: The first record's tag list is the template's tag list, so appending to it contaminates the template, and the second record inherits a tag that belongs to someone else.

candidate.test_code:

print(repr(result))
assert result == ["a", "b"], "the snapshot should not see a row added after it was taken"
candidate.context_note: Records what a list held before a change, so the two can be compared afterwards.

reason_options.text: Returning the list hands back the same object, so the snapshot and the live list are two names for one thing and both show the later addition
append() returns a new list, so append_row() mutates a temporary and the original is untouched
A function cannot return its own argument, so snapshot() returns None
Dictionary values are copied when the dictionary is built, so before is already independent by the time audit() returns

draft_explanation.summary: Returning an argument returns the object, not a copy of it. The snapshot is a second name for the very list that is about to be appended to, so by the time anyone reads it, it shows the change it was supposed to predate.
draft_explanation.principle: Returning a mutable argument shares it. To capture a value at a moment in time, copy it.

line_notes.note: This hands back the same object, not a copy of it.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: The list is mutated after the snapshot is taken.
candidate.divergence_input: audit(["a", "b"]), reading the before entry after a row is appended.
candidate.divergence_justification: The snapshot and the live list are the same object, so the row appended afterwards appears in the record of what the list held beforehand.

candidate.test_code:
handle("warmup", 99)
result = handle("db", 5)["spans"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == {"db": 5}, "a new request should start with no spans"
candidate.context_note: Collects the timing of each step of a request, so the slowest can be reported.

reason_options.text: The empty dictionary is built once, when the function is defined, so every request that does not pass one appends to the same shared object
A dictionary cannot be used as a default argument, so Python rebuilds it on every call
sorted() on items() yields keys only, so slowest() ranks the span names
Assigning spans inside the loop rebinds the caller's name, so only the last step survives

draft_explanation.summary: A default argument is evaluated once, when the def statement runs. The dictionary therefore belongs to the function, not to the call, and every request that omits it writes into the same object. Yesterday's spans turn up in today's trace.
draft_explanation.principle: Default arguments are evaluated at definition time. Never default to a mutable object.

line_notes.note: This dictionary is built once, when the module is imported, and shared by every call.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A second request that does not pass its own span collection.
candidate.divergence_input: One request tracing a warmup step, then a second request tracing a db step.
candidate.divergence_justification: The first request's warmup span is still sitting in the shared default when the second request begins, so it is reported as part of the second request.

candidate.test_code:

reason_options.text: range() stops before its second argument, so the last day of an inclusive window is never in the list
range() starts at zero regardless of its first argument, so the window is shifted
Membership testing with in on a list raises TypeError when the list holds integers
append() inside the loop overwrites the previous entry, so days only ever holds one value

draft_explanation.summary: The window is documented as inclusive of both ends, but range() stops one short of its upper argument. The final day is quietly outside the window, so every event on the last day of every window is dropped.
draft_explanation.principle: range(a, b) stops before b. An inclusive upper bound needs b plus one.

line_notes.note: This stops one short of the last day the window is meant to include.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: An event that falls on the final day of the window.
candidate.divergence_input: Events on day one and day three, with a window of day one to day three.
candidate.divergence_justification: The window list is [1, 2], so the event on day three falls outside it and is dropped even though the window is meant to include that day.
